title: Decision Card
description: A compact, high-leverage human-gate prompt. When a skill reaches a point that genuinely needs a human decision, it emits a decision card instead of handing the user a long artifact to synthesize alone. The card states the decision, the assistant's recommendation, the risk of getting it wrong, and the one action to approve — so the intervention is short and concrete. The card SUMMARIZES the artifact; it does not replace reading it when review_required is true.

gate_id.description: Stable identifier for this gate, e.g. 'narrative-spine-approval' or 'submission-summary-confirmation'.

generated_by.description: The skill that emitted the card.

decision.description: The decision the human must make, in one sentence.

artifact.description: Path to the full artifact this card summarizes — the thing the human should open when review_required is true.

recommendation.description: The assistant's recommended answer to the decision.

confidence.description: The assistant's confidence in the recommendation. Low confidence means the human should not rubber-stamp.

rationale.description: Why the recommendation, in one or two sentences.

risk_if_wrong.description: What goes wrong downstream if the human approves a bad answer. The most important field — it tells the human how hard to look.

on_approval.description: What happens next if the card is approved.

human_time_estimate.description: Honest estimate of the human time the gate needs, e.g. '3 minutes'.

approve_action.description: The single command or action that approves and continues, e.g. '/proposal-storyboard'.

review_required.description: TRUE when the human must actually read the artifact before approving, not merely glance at the card. The narrative-spine gate is always review_required:true — the one-page spine decides the whole proposal's argument and is the cheapest, highest-leverage point to correct course. The card frames that read; it never invites skipping it.
